title: Bhrigu Lake Trek 2026 — 3N/4D from Manali
meta_description: Bhrigu Lake Trek 2026 — 3N/4D from Manali, high-altitude lake at 4,300m, 360° panorama of Rohtang and Kullu peaks. Easy-Moderate. From ₹9,500.
keywords: Bhrigu Lake trek, Bhrigu Lake trek Manali, Bhrigu Lake altitude, Bhrigu Lake 2026, Himachal Pradesh high altitude lake trek, Manali trek, Rohtang trek
hero_tagline: A sacred alpine lake at 4,300m — the holy bathing spot of sage Bhrigu with Rohtang's giants all around
destinations_short: Manali · Gulaba · Rola Kholi · Bhrigu Lake
wa_text: Bhrigu Lake Trek enquiry chahiye
overview:
The Bhrigu Lake Trek reaches one of Himachal Pradesh's most sacred and spectacular high-altitude lakes in just 3 nights and 4 days from Manali. The lake sits at 4,300 metres in an open alpine bowl with a 360-degree panorama of the Rohtang Pass ridge, Deo Tibba, and the vast Kullu valley below. According to Hindu legend, the great sage Bhrigu used this crystal-clear lake for bathing, and local communities still conduct annual rituals here.

The approach from Gulaba (on the Manali–Rohtang road) climbs through rhododendron meadows and open grassland, passing the beautiful intermediate camp at Rola Kholi. Unlike many Himachal treks that require long valley walks to reach the alpine zone, Bhrigu Lake gets you into open meadows and mountain views within hours of leaving the trailhead. The lake itself is often partially frozen even in July, and the reflections of the surrounding peaks on calm mornings are extraordinary.

This is an ideal trek for those who want a genuine high-altitude lake experience without the length and commitment of Hamta Pass or Roopkund. It is graded Easy to Moderate and suitable for first-time high-altitude trekkers. Best season: June to early October.

itinerary.title: Day 1 — Manali to Moridugh via Kulang
itinerary.meta: Drive 12 km to Kulang · Trek to Moridugh · Overnight camp
itinerary.desc: Your Junegiri Yatra adventure begins with a short drive from Manali to the village of Kulang, roughly 12 km away. From Kulang, the trek sets off through dense forests, crossing Chor Nallah and Kohli Nallah at several points as the trail climbs steadily up the mountain. You'll carry a packed lunch along the way, allowing you to pause and soak in the forest silence before settling into camp. The evening at Moridugh is yours to explore the campsite surroundings, acclimatize to the altitude, and end the night with breathtaking stargazing under an open Himalayan sky.
highlights: Drive from Manali to Kulang trailhead
Forest trail crossing Chor Nallah and Kohli Nallah
Arrival and acclimatization at Moridugh campsite
Stargazing from alpine tents at high altitude

itinerary.title: Day 2 — Moridugh to Bhrigu Lake Summit and Back
itinerary.meta: Trek to Bhrigu Lake (14,100 ft) · Summit day · Overnight camp at Moridugh
itinerary.desc: Rise early for the most rewarding day of the trek — the ascent to Bhrigu Lake at 14,100 feet. After breakfast, the trail winds through a silver oak woodland for the first couple of hours before breaking open into sweeping alpine meadows above 10,000 feet. The views here are stunning, with panoramas of the Seven Sister Peaks, Deo Tibba, Indrasan, Hanuman Tibba, and the Pir Panjal ranges stretching across the horizon. At the lake, the oval-shaped glacial basin — sacred to the sage Bhrigu — reflects the sky in shades of emerald and blue. Enjoy packed lunch by the shore, capture the scenery, and descend back to Moridugh for a well-earned dinner and overnight rest.
highlights: Ascent through silver oak forest to alpine meadows
Bhrigu Lake summit at 14,100 feet
Panoramic views of Deo Tibba, Indrasan, and Pir Panjal ranges
Glacial lake visit — sacred site of Sage Bhrigu

itinerary.title: Day 3 — Moridugh Descent to Kulang and Drive to Manali
itinerary.meta: Trek descent to Kulang · Drive back to Manali
itinerary.desc: After a hearty breakfast at camp, the team packs up and begins the descent from Moridugh back down to Kulang village, retracing the forested trail from Day 1 with fresh eyes and a sense of accomplishment. The downhill walk offers a chance to pause at viewpoints you may have rushed past on the ascent. From Kulang, a vehicle arranged by Junegiri Yatra completes the return journey to Manali, bringing a memorable mountain escape to a satisfying close.
highlights: Descend from Moridugh through forest trail to Kulang
Final views of the Kullu valley landscape
Drive back to Manali from Kulang village
Trek concludes at Manali

faq.q: What is the altitude of Bhrigu Lake?
faq.a: Bhrigu Lake sits at 4,300 metres (14,107 feet) above sea level. This is a genuine high-altitude trek and mild altitude sickness symptoms (headache, fatigue) are possible above 3,500 metres. Good hydration, a slow pace, and listening to your guide are the key precautions. Most trekkers acclimatise naturally given the gradual approach profile.

faq.q: Is Bhrigu Lake suitable for beginners?
faq.a: Yes, Bhrigu Lake is graded Easy to Moderate and is suitable for first-time high-altitude trekkers with reasonable fitness. The total trek distance is manageable (around 20 km over 3 days) and the trail is well-defined. 3–4 weeks of fitness preparation (daily walks of 5–8 km) is recommended.

faq.q: Can I do Bhrigu Lake and Hamta Pass in the same trip?
faq.a: Yes — this is a popular combination. Do Bhrigu Lake (3N/4D) first as an acclimatisation trek, then Hamta Pass (4N/5D) for the main crossing. Total trip: 7–8 days from Manali. Junegiri Yatra can arrange a combined itinerary with special pricing. WhatsApp us to enquire.

faq.q: Is the lake frozen in summer?
faq.a: Bhrigu Lake is often partially frozen until late June or early July, even in summer. In June, the lake may be 60–70% frozen; by August, usually fully thawed. The combination of blue water and white ice is particularly photogenic in late June to early July. By September the lake is fully open.

travel_guide:
Complete Guide to Bhrigu Lake Trek from Manali

Bhrigu Lake is one of Himachal Pradesh's most accessible high-altitude lakes, reaching 4,300 metres in just two days of trekking from the Gulaba trailhead on the Manali–Rohtang road. The combination of alpine meadow walking, a sacred lake, and a hot spring recovery makes this one of the most satisfying short treks in north India.

How to Reach Manali

Manali is connected to Delhi by overnight Volvo bus (14 hours), by flights to Bhuntar Airport (50 km away), and by train to Chandigarh or Pathankot followed by a bus. Junegiri Yatra provides transport from Manali to the Gulaba trailhead as part of all Bhrigu Lake packages.

Significance of Bhrigu Lake

According to Hindu mythology, the great sage Bhrigu — author of the Bhrigu Samhita — used this lake for bathing and meditation. The lake is considered sacred by local communities, and a small religious ceremony is held each year at the lakeshore. Many trekkers perform a ritual circumambulation (parikrama) of the lake in keeping with local tradition. The lake's pristine condition is partly due to this religious reverence — littering near the lake is deeply frowned upon by local guides and communities alike.

title: Kareri Lake Trek 2026 — 3N/4D Dharamshala

keywords: Kareri Lake trek, Kareri Lake trek Dharamshala, Kareri Lake McLeod Ganj, Kareri trek 2026, easy Himachal trek, Dharamshala trek, alpine lake trek
hero_tagline: A hidden gem above Dharamshala — emerald forests, river crossings, and a pristine alpine lake
destinations_short: McLeod Ganj · Kareri Village · Ravi River · Kareri Lake
wa_text: Kareri Lake Trek enquiry chahiye
overview:
The Kareri Lake Trek is Dharamshala's best-kept trekking secret — a 3-night, 4-day journey that takes you from the vibrant lanes of McLeod Ganj into a deeply peaceful forest landscape, past roaring river crossings and traditional Gaddi shepherd villages, to a pristine alpine lake nestled under the towering Dhauladhar range at 2,950 metres. Unlike the more crowded Triund ridge, Kareri draws a quieter, more adventurous crowd — and the reward is a lake so clear you can see the bottom, ringed by peaks and rhododendron slopes.

The approach begins from Kareri village, a small cluster of traditional Himachali stone houses an hour's drive from McLeod Ganj. The trail follows the Nyund Nallah stream, crossing it multiple times on log bridges and stepping stones — part of the trek's signature charm. The forest canopy of oak, maple, and rhododendron is spectacular in spring (April–May) when the rhododendrons bloom crimson, and in autumn (October) when the maples turn gold.

The lake itself is a natural glacial bowl at 2,950 metres, almost perfectly circular, with the Dhauladhar peaks reflected on its surface on calm mornings. In early spring the lake is partially frozen; by May it is fully open and the surrounding meadows are carpeted in wildflowers. The camping ground on the lake shore is flat and well-maintained, with a small Forest Department hut nearby. On a clear night, the Milky Way stretches across the sky without a trace of light pollution.

Junegiri Yatra manages all logistics: transport from McLeod Ganj to Kareri village, camping equipment including -5°C sleeping bags, all trail meals, a certified local guide, and Himachal Pradesh forest permits. The trek is graded Easy to Moderate — the river crossings add some challenge but the altitude gain is gradual and manageable. Best season: April to June and September to November.

itinerary.title: Day 1 — McLeod Ganj to Kareri Village, Trek to Camp
itinerary.meta: Drive 14 km to Kareri village + Trek 4 km / Altitude gain to 1,980 m / 2–3 hrs
itinerary.desc: Drive from McLeod Ganj to Kareri village (1,800m), a charming cluster of traditional Gaddi shepherd homes on a wooded hillside. Begin the trek along the Nyund Nallah stream, crossing it twice on log bridges. The forest is dense and birdsong-filled — watch for Himalayan birds including the blue-capped rock thrush. Set up camp by the stream at 1,980m in time for a hot dinner.
highlights: Traditional Gaddi village architecture and culture
First river crossings on log bridges — exciting and scenic
Camp beside the Nyund Nallah stream in dense oak forest

itinerary.title: Day 2 — Camp to Kareri Lake
itinerary.meta: Trek 8 km / Altitude gain to 2,950 m / 6–7 hrs
itinerary.desc: The main trekking day. The trail climbs steadily through increasingly open terrain, with the river crossings becoming more frequent and more thrilling. Above the treeline, the valley opens into a wide alpine meadow and the Kareri Lake basin reveals itself — a blue-green oval of still water reflecting the Dhauladhar peaks. Set up camp on the lake shore and spend the afternoon exploring, fishing, or simply sitting in the extraordinary silence.
highlights: Multiple river crossings — the highlight of the Kareri trail
Above-treeline meadows with Dhauladhar views
Kareri Lake — pristine, circular, and utterly peaceful at 2,950m

itinerary.title: Day 3 — Rest Day at Kareri Lake
itinerary.meta: Optional exploration / Rest and acclimatisation
itinerary.desc: A full day at the lake. Optional activities include a climb to the ridge above the lake for panoramic views of the Dhauladhar range and down into the Kangra valley, or a circuit walk around the lake shore. Many guests simply rest, read, take photographs, and absorb the extraordinary mountain silence. This extra day separates Kareri from most short treks — it allows genuine immersion in the high-altitude environment.
highlights: Optional ridge walk above the lake for 360° Dhauladhar panorama
Lake circumambulation walk with reflections
Wildlife watching — monal pheasant and musk deer sightings common

itinerary.title: Day 4 — Kareri Lake to McLeod Ganj
itinerary.meta: Trek 12 km descent + Drive / 5–6 hrs total
itinerary.desc: Begin the descent early to enjoy the morning light on the lake before leaving. The downhill trek retraces the outward route and takes 4–5 hours at a comfortable pace. Reach Kareri village by early afternoon, board the vehicle, and return to McLeod Ganj. Celebrate in the cafés of McLeod Ganj with momos and chai. Trek concludes.
highlights: Last morning light on Kareri Lake — golden reflections
Descending river crossings in reverse — just as fun
McLeod Ganj return for Tibetan food and culture

testimonials.quote: Kareri Lake completely blew me away. I'd done Triund and thought Dharamshala was 'done' — Kareri is on another level. The river crossings are a blast and the lake is absolutely pristine. Very well organised by Junegiri Yatra.
testimonials.author: Nandini Sharma

testimonials.quote: The rest day at the lake was the best decision. We climbed the ridge behind the lake and could see the Kangra valley stretching all the way to the plains. Guide was fantastic and the camping food was surprisingly great.
testimonials.author: Arjun & Priya Mehta

faq.q: How difficult is the Kareri Lake Trek?
faq.a: Kareri Lake is graded Easy to Moderate. The trail itself is not technically demanding, but the multiple river crossings require balance and confidence. The total distance is around 24 km over 3 days of trekking, with a gradual altitude gain to 2,950 metres. Anyone with reasonable fitness can complete this trek. A 3–4 week fitness preparation of daily walking is recommended.

faq.q: When is the best time for Kareri Lake Trek?
faq.a: The two best windows are April–June and September–November. April–May offers rhododendron blooms and the lake transitioning from frozen to open. October has crisp visibility and the chance of light snow dusting on the Dhauladhar ridge. July–August are avoided as monsoon rains make the river crossings dangerous and the trail very slippery.

faq.q: Are the river crossings safe?
faq.a: Yes — Junegiri Yatra guides are trained in safe river crossing techniques and assist every trekker at each crossing. Crossings are on stepping stones or log bridges depending on water levels. In high water conditions (late June after heavy rains), we use trekking poles and guide ropes. July–August crossings can be genuinely dangerous and we do not operate in that window.

faq.q: Can I combine Kareri Lake with a Triund Trek?
faq.a: Yes — a popular 5-day Dharamshala combo does Triund on Day 1–2 and Kareri on Day 3–5. Junegiri Yatra offers this as a custom itinerary. WhatsApp us for combined pricing and logistics.

faq.q: Is fishing allowed at Kareri Lake?
faq.a: Kareri Lake has a small population of brown trout. Fishing requires a permit from the Himachal Pradesh Fisheries Department, which can be arranged in advance through Junegiri Yatra. Catch-and-release is encouraged to preserve the ecosystem. The lake is a protected area under the Dhauladhar Wildlife Sanctuary.

faq.q: What accommodation is there at Kareri Lake?
faq.a: There is a small Forest Department rest house at the lake that can be booked in advance (limited availability). Junegiri Yatra provides high-quality dome tents with sleeping bags rated to -5°C. There are no permanent lodges or tea stalls at the lake — you are in true wilderness camping territory.

travel_guide:
Complete Guide to Kareri Lake Trek from Dharamshala

Kareri Lake sits in the Dhauladhar Wildlife Sanctuary above Dharamshala, at the base of the sheer rock wall that defines the Dhauladhar range. The lake feeds the Nyund Nallah stream, which in turn flows through the forested gorge that makes up the approach trail. The combination of lush lowland forest, multiple river crossings, and a pristine high-altitude lake gives Kareri a character entirely distinct from other Himachal treks of similar length and difficulty.

How to Reach Dharamshala / McLeod Ganj

McLeod Ganj is connected to Delhi by overnight Volvo bus (12 hours), to Chandigarh by bus (5 hours), and by flights to Gaggal/Kangra Airport (25 km). Junegiri Yatra provides private transport from McLeod Ganj to the Kareri village trailhead as part of all packages.

Flora and Wildlife on the Kareri Trail

The forest below 2,500 metres is a mix of banj oak, rhododendron, and maple — spectacular in April when the rhododendrons bloom red and the oak is bright with new leaves. Above 2,500 metres the vegetation transitions to alpine meadow with Himalayan blue poppy, primrose, and gentian. The sanctuary protects snow leopard (rare sightings), musk deer, Himalayan black bear, and a rich birdlife including the Himalayan monal, blue-capped rock thrush, and lammergeier vulture.

Sustainable Trekking at Kareri Lake

Kareri Lake sits within the Dhauladhar Wildlife Sanctuary and the forest department has strict rules about campfires, littering, and noise levels near the lake. Junegiri Yatra operates on full Leave No Trace principles — all waste is carried out, campfires are only made at designated spots (or gas stoves used instead), and group sizes are kept small. By trekking with a responsible operator, you directly support the preservation of this irreplaceable ecosystem.

name: Beas Kund Trek 2N/3D
title: Beas Kund Trek 2026 — 2N/3D from Manali
meta_description: Beas Kund Trek 2026 — 2N/3D from Manali. Source of the Beas river, glacial lake at 3,700m, wildflower meadows and Shitidhar peak views. Easy. From ₹6,500.
keywords: Beas Kund trek, Beas Kund Manali, Beas Kund trek 2026, source of Beas river, Manali glacier trek, easy Himachal trek, Dhundi trek, Bakarthach trek
hero_tagline: Stand at the source of the mighty Beas river — a glacial lake ringed by peaks, just 2 nights from Manali
wa_text: Beas Kund Trek enquiry chahiye
overview:
The Beas Kund Trek is Manali's most rewarding easy escape — a 2-night, 3-day journey to a glacial lake at 3,700 metres that holds a place of both natural beauty and mythological significance. This is the source of the Beas river, one of the five great rivers of Punjab, and according to legend the spot where the sage Vyas (Vyasa, author of the Mahabharata) bathed after completing that monumental work. The kund (pool or tank) is a still, brilliantly blue glacial lake fed directly by the Shitidhar glacier — the snout of the glacier is clearly visible a short distance above the lake.

The approach from Dhundi (on the Manali–Rohtang road) climbs through one of the finest wildflower meadows in Himachal Pradesh. In July and August, the meadow at Bakarthach (the base camp at 3,200 metres) is carpeted in blue primrose, yellow potentilla, purple aconite, and dozens of other alpine species. The views of the Hanuman Tibba (5,982 m), Shitidhar, and the entire Rohtang–Kullu ridge are outstanding from the trail.

The trek is graded Easy — the trail is well-defined, the altitude gain is gradual, and no technical skills are required. It is an ideal first Himalayan trek for families, beginners, or those who want a short but genuine mountain wilderness experience close to Manali. The trek is also popular as an acclimatisation outing for those planning the Hamta Pass Trek or more serious Himachal objectives.

Junegiri Yatra includes private transport from Manali to Dhundi, all camping equipment, a certified guide, and all meals on the mountain. The season runs from June to September, with July and August being the wildflower peak.

itinerary.title: Day 1 — Manali to Dhundi, Trek to Bakarthach
itinerary.meta: Drive 14 km + Trek 6 km / Altitude gain to 3,200 m / 4–5 hrs
itinerary.desc: Drive from Manali along the Rohtang road to Dhundi (2,900 m), a small settlement at the end of the road beyond Solang Nallah. The trek begins immediately in open meadow terrain with wide views of the Kullu valley. Climb steadily through increasing wildflower density to reach Bakarthach — a broad, flat meadow camp at 3,200 metres under the shadow of Hanuman Tibba. Set up camp, acclimatise, and enjoy the sunset over the peaks.
highlights: Immediate open meadow trekking from Dhundi — no forest slog
First views of Hanuman Tibba (5,982m) and Shitidhar peak
Bakarthach camp surrounded by wildflowers in July–August

itinerary.title: Day 2 — Bakarthach to Beas Kund and Return
itinerary.meta: Trek 10 km up and back / Max altitude 3,700 m / 6–7 hrs
itinerary.desc: The summit day. The trail from Bakarthach climbs through more open meadow and then moraine to the Beas Kund. The glacial lake appears almost suddenly — a brilliant blue-green pool beneath the dirty white snout of the Shitidhar glacier. The silence and scale here is extraordinary. Spend time at the lake, take photographs, and then descend back to Bakarthach for the night. For those who want more, a climb to the ridge above the kund gives views into the Rohtang plateau.
highlights: Beas Kund — source of the sacred Beas river at 3,700m
Shitidhar glacier snout visible directly above the lake
Optional ridge climb for Rohtang plateau views

itinerary.title: Day 3 — Bakarthach to Manali
itinerary.meta: Trek 6 km descent + Drive / 3–4 hrs total
itinerary.desc: Descend from Bakarthach to Dhundi in the clear morning air. The downhill is quick and comfortable. Board the vehicle at Dhundi and drive back to Manali. The day is yours — explore Old Manali, visit Hadimba Temple, or prepare for onwards travel. Trek concludes.
highlights: Morning meadow descent with Kullu valley panorama
Final views of Hanuman Tibba and Rohtang ridge
Manali return — Hadimba Temple and Old Manali to explore

testimonials.quote: Perfect first trek. We did Beas Kund and it completely changed how I think about mountains. The glacial lake at 3,700m is jaw-dropping. Two nights is the right amount — enough to really feel you're in the wilderness.
testimonials.author: Sanjay Kapoor

testimonials.quote: The wildflowers at Bakarthach in July are something else entirely. We spent the afternoon sitting in the meadow with Hanuman Tibba above us — nothing else like it. Junegiri Yatra made it effortless.
testimonials.author: Preet Kaur

faq.q: Is Beas Kund Trek suitable for complete beginners?
faq.a: Yes — Beas Kund is one of the easiest high-altitude treks in Himachal Pradesh and is ideal for first-timers. The trail is straightforward, the altitude gain is gradual, and no technical skills are needed. It is also popular with families with children from age 10 upwards. A basic level of fitness (comfortable walking 8–10 km per day) is sufficient.

faq.q: What is the altitude of Beas Kund?
faq.a: Beas Kund sits at 3,700 metres (12,139 feet) above sea level. The base camp at Bakarthach is at 3,200 metres. The altitude gain over two days is gradual and well-paced, giving your body time to acclimatise naturally. Stay well hydrated (3 litres/day on trek) and tell your guide immediately if you experience headaches or nausea.

faq.q: Can I combine Beas Kund with Hamta Pass Trek?
faq.a: Yes — Beas Kund (2N/3D) is an excellent warm-up and acclimatisation trek before Hamta Pass (4N/5D). Combined total: 6 days from Manali. Junegiri Yatra offers combined packages with special pricing. WhatsApp us for the combined itinerary.

faq.q: Is there any snow on the Beas Kund trail?
faq.a: In June, there may be snow patches above 3,400m. By July the trail is snow-free up to Bakarthach, with the glacier snout visible above the kund. The glacier itself is always present above the lake. Snow on the trail is not expected July–September.

faq.q: What is the significance of Beas Kund?
faq.a: Beas Kund is the source of the Beas (Vipasha) River, one of the five rivers of Punjab. In Hindu tradition, the lake is associated with the sage Vyasa (Vyas), who is said to have bathed here after completing the Mahabharata. 'Kund' means sacred pool or water body. The lake is revered by local Gaddi shepherds and a small ritual is performed by some trekkers and guides at the water's edge.

travel_guide:
Complete Guide to Beas Kund Trek from Manali

Beas Kund is reached from the Dhundi trailhead on the Manali–Rohtang highway, roughly 14 km from Manali town. The route passes through the Solang Nallah valley — famous as Manali's adventure sports hub — before turning up into the quieter Bakarthach valley. The contrast between the crowded Solang chairlift and the pristine wilderness of Bakarthach meadow, just two hours above, is one of the great pleasures of this trek.

How to Reach Manali

Manali is connected to Delhi by overnight Volvo bus (14 hours), by flights to Bhuntar Airport (50 km), and by train to Chandigarh or Pathankot followed by a bus. Junegiri Yatra provides private transport from Manali to the Dhundi trailhead and back as part of all packages.

Beas Kund as an Acclimatisation Trek

For trekkers planning more demanding objectives — Hamta Pass (4,270m), Bhrigu Lake (4,300m), or Deo Tibba base camp — Beas Kund is the ideal acclimatisation warm-up. Two nights at 3,200m and a day-trip to 3,700m puts your body in excellent shape for the next challenge. Many Junegiri Yatra guests combine Beas Kund (2N/3D) with Hamta Pass (4N/5D) for a perfectly structured 6-day Manali adventure.

name: Indrahar Pass Trek 3N/4D
title: Indrahar Pass Trek 2026 — 3N/4D McLeod Ganj
meta_description: Book Indrahar Pass Trek 2026 with Junegiri Yatra. 3N/4D, cross the Dhauladhar range from McLeod Ganj at 4,342m. See Pir Panjal and Dhauladhar simultaneously.…
keywords: Indrahar Pass trek, Indrahar Pass from McLeod Ganj, Dhauladhar trek, Triund Indrahar Pass, Himachal Pradesh trek 2026, Lahesh Cave trek, Dharamshala trek
hero_tagline: Cross the mighty Dhauladhar range from McLeod Ganj — views of two great Himalayan sub-ranges from a single pass
destinations_short: McLeod Ganj · Triund · Lahesh Cave · Indrahar Pass
departure: Apr–Jun, Sep–Nov departures from McLeod Ganj
wa_text: Indrahar Pass Trek enquiry chahiye
overview:
The Indrahar Pass Trek is one of the most rewarding crossings in the Dhauladhar range of Himachal Pradesh. Starting from the vibrant McLeod Ganj — the Dalai Lama's residence in exile — the trail climbs steeply through oak and rhododendron forests to the iconic Triund meadow before continuing higher to the rarely visited Lahesh Cave and ultimately the Indrahar Pass at 4,342 metres (14,245 ft).

What makes Indrahar special is the dual panorama from the pass. Standing on the summit, you see the lush green Kangra Valley and the plains of Punjab stretching behind you to the south, while ahead lies the snowbound spine of the Pir Panjal range — the same peaks you see from Shimla. On a clear day, the contrast between the subtropical foothills below and the cold alpine world around you is almost surreal.

The trek follows a classic acclimatisation arc: Triund (2,875m) on Day 1 → Lahesh Cave (3,400m) on Day 2 → Indrahar Pass (4,342m) on Day 3. This steady altitude gain allows the body to adjust without rushing. The Lahesh Cave camp — a large overhanging rock shelter used by Gaddi shepherds for centuries — is one of the most atmospheric overnight spots in all of Himachal Pradesh.

Junegiri Yatra manages all logistics: transport from Haridwar or Chandigarh to McLeod Ganj, certified Dhauladhar trek guides, camping equipment, and all meals on trail. The best seasons are April to June and September to November — July and August are possible but wet underfoot. This trek suits anyone who has completed at least one multi-day trek before; it is not recommended as a first trek due to the altitude and steep sections above Lahesh Cave.

itinerary.title: Day 1 — McLeod Ganj to Triund
itinerary.meta: Trek 9 km / Altitude gain to 2,875 m / 4–5 hrs
itinerary.desc: After an early morning briefing in McLeod Ganj, the trail begins at Dharamkot and climbs through forest to the Magic View Café before opening into the famous Triund meadow. The last kilometre is steep — 22 hairpin switchbacks — but the payoff is enormous: a vast flat meadow with the Dhauladhar wall rising above and the Kangra Valley spread below. Camp at Triund, acclimatise, and enjoy your first high-altitude sunset.
highlights: Dharamkot forest trail with Himalayan oak and rhododendron
Triund meadow — one of Himachal's most celebrated viewpoints
Full Dhauladhar wall and Kangra Valley panorama

itinerary.title: Day 2 — Triund to Lahesh Cave
itinerary.meta: Trek 6 km / Altitude gain to 3,400 m / 4–5 hrs
itinerary.desc: The trail climbs steadily above the treeline from Triund, passing through rocky alpine terrain. Snow patches are common above 3,100m even in summer. Lahesh Cave is a massive overhanging rock that provides natural shelter and has been used by Gaddi nomadic shepherds for generations. The cave can shelter 15–20 people. Camp beside or below the cave. This altitude requires an early night and good hydration — the pass attempt tomorrow starts at 4am.
highlights: Above-treeline rocky terrain with snow patches
Lahesh Cave — ancient Gaddi shepherd shelter at 3,400 m
Increasingly dramatic views of the Dhauladhar peaks

itinerary.title: Day 3 — Lahesh Cave to Indrahar Pass and Return
itinerary.meta: Trek 5 km to pass + descent / Max altitude 4,342 m / 8–9 hrs
itinerary.desc: A 4am start in the dark, following head-torch light up steep boulder fields and snow slopes to the Indrahar Pass at 4,342 metres. At the top, the Pir Panjal range reveals itself on the far side — a sea of snow-covered peaks stretching toward Lahul and Spiti. Spend time at the pass before descending carefully back to Lahesh Cave or continuing to Chamba district on the far side (Chamba crossing is a separate full-day itinerary). Return trekkers descend to Triund for the final night.
highlights: Indrahar Pass summit at 4,342 m — dual panorama of Dhauladhar and Pir Panjal
Pre-dawn alpine ascent on snow and boulder terrain
Option to cross to Chamba district (multi-day extension available)

itinerary.title: Day 4 — Triund Descent to McLeod Ganj
itinerary.meta: Trek 9 km / Descent to 1,450 m / 3–4 hrs
itinerary.desc: After a relaxed morning in Triund watching the sunrise paint the Dhauladhar peaks, descend to McLeod Ganj via the same trail or an alternate forest path through Gallu Devi Temple. Back in McLeod Ganj by midday — celebration lunch, hot shower, and transfer to Dharamshala bus stand or onward destination.
highlights: Final sunrise views over the Kangra Valley from Triund
Descent through deodar and oak forest
Trek concludes in McLeod Ganj

faqs.q: How difficult is the Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.a: Indrahar Pass is graded Moderate–Difficult. It is significantly harder than Triund (which is Easy–Moderate) due to the steep, exposed terrain above Lahesh Cave and the 4am pre-dawn ascent on snow slopes to the pass. Prior trekking experience at altitude is recommended. The descent is also demanding on the knees. That said, with a good guide and a steady pace, fit and motivated trekkers who have done one or two previous treks can complete it comfortably.

faqs.q: What is the best time for the Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.a: The best windows are April–June and September–November. In April–May, there is snow on the upper sections which adds drama but requires microspikes. June is usually the clearest month. September–October has superb visibility and dry trails. Avoid the monsoon months (July–August) when trails above Triund become slippery and cloud cover can make the pass crossing dangerous. The trek is closed November to March.

faqs.q: Is Triund a separate trek I can do on the way?
faqs.a: Yes — Triund is the first night camp on the Indrahar Pass Trek (Day 1). For those who want just Triund, Junegiri Yatra offers a standalone Triund overnight trek (1N/2D). The Indrahar Pass Trek essentially starts where the Triund experience ends, going higher and deeper into the Dhauladhar range. Many trekkers do Triund first as a warm-up and then return for the full Indrahar Pass crossing.

testimonials.quote: Standing on Indrahar Pass with the Pir Panjal range unfolding in front of me is something I cannot describe in words. The 4am climb in the dark with head torches was eerie and magical. Junegiri guide Ranjeet was exceptional — calm, knowledgeable, and careful about pace.
testimonials.author: Kavya Menon

testimonials.quote: Did Indrahar Pass in October — perfect conditions. Lahesh Cave overnight was unforgettable, woke up to frost on the tent and completely clear skies. The Kangra Valley view from Triund on the way down is one of India's great views. 100% worth the effort.
testimonials.author: Sumit Rawat

name: Chandrakhani Pass Trek 3N/4D
title: Chandrakhani Pass Trek 2026 — 3N/4D
meta_description: Book Chandrakhani Pass Trek 2026. 3N/4D from Naggar, Kullu Valley. Cross at 3,660m through apple orchards and deodar forest to mysterious Malana village.…
keywords: Chandrakhani Pass trek, Chandrakhani Pass Naggar, Malana village trek, Kullu Valley trek, apple orchard trek Himachal, Chandrakhani Pass 2026, Naggar castle trek
hero_tagline: Through Kullu Valley's apple orchards and ancient deodar forests to the enigmatic Malana village
destinations_short: Naggar · Rumsu · Chandrakhani Pass · Malana · Kasol
wa_text: Chandrakhani Pass Trek enquiry chahiye
overview:
The Chandrakhani Pass Trek is Kullu Valley's best-kept trekking secret — a route that winds through fragrant apple orchards, old-growth deodar cedar forests, and high alpine meadows before descending to the famously insular Malana village. At 3,660 metres (12,008 ft), Chandrakhani is one of the lower passes in the Kullu region, making it accessible to moderate-level trekkers while still delivering the dramatic landscape change of a genuine Himalayan crossing.

The trek begins at Rumsu village near Naggar — itself worth a visit for its ancient stone castle and Nicholas Roerich Art Gallery. The trail immediately enters apple and plum orchards that frame the route for the first hour, a uniquely beautiful start unlike most Himalayan treks. Above the orchards, the deodar and oak forest gives way to open meadows with views of Deo Tibba (6,001m) and the Pir Panjal range to the north.

The pass crossing itself is a broad, relatively gentle saddle with 360-degree views of the Kullu Valley on one side and the remote Malana Valley on the other. The descent into Malana is dramatic — steep switchbacks through rhododendron forest dropping into one of India's most distinctive villages. Malana has its own dialect, customs, and ancient democratic governance system. Visitors are welcome but must respect the local rules: do not touch villagers or their property.

From Malana, the route continues to Kasol in the Parvati Valley — a popular backpacker hub — and then back to Manali by road. Junegiri Yatra manages the full logistics: transport, certified guides, camping, and all meals on trail.

itinerary.title: Day 1 — Manali to Naggar, Drive to Rumsu, Camp
itinerary.meta: Drive 22 km to Naggar, short walk to Rumsu / Altitude 1,800 m
itinerary.desc: Drive from Manali along the left bank of the Beas River to Naggar. Optional visit to Naggar Castle (14th-century stone fortress) and the Roerich Art Gallery. Drive or short walk up to Rumsu village (1,800m) through apple and plum orchards. Set up camp at the village edge with first views of the Kullu Valley panorama.
highlights: Naggar Castle — 14th century Kullu kingdom fortress
Nicholas Roerich Art Gallery
Rumsu village and apple orchard arrival

itinerary.title: Day 2 — Rumsu to Chandrakhani Pass and Meadow Camp
itinerary.meta: Trek 8 km / Altitude gain to 3,660 m / 6–7 hrs
itinerary.desc: The main trekking day. The trail climbs steeply through deodar and oak forest before breaking into open meadows. At the Chandrakhani Pass (3,660m), the views are superb in all directions — the Kullu Valley floor far below, the Parvati and Malana valleys ahead, and the snow-capped peaks of the Pir Panjal and Dhauladhar ranges. Camp just below or beyond the pass in the high meadow.
highlights: Chandrakhani Pass at 3,660 m — panoramic views
Deo Tibba (6,001 m) and Pir Panjal range in full view
Transition from Kullu Valley to Malana Valley

itinerary.title: Day 3 — Meadow Descent toward Malana Village
itinerary.meta: Trek 6 km / Descent to 2,650 m / 4–5 hrs
itinerary.desc: Descend through rhododendron forest and dramatic cliff-side trails into the Malana Valley. Arrive at the outskirts of Malana village — one of India's most isolated and culturally distinct communities, with its own language (Kanashi) and ancient democratic parliament. Guided village walk respecting local customs (no touching people or property). Camp near the village.
highlights: Steep descent through rhododendron and pine forest
Malana village — ancient self-governing community
Cultural interaction and guided village walk

itinerary.title: Day 4 — Malana to Kasol, Drive to Manali
itinerary.meta: Trek 3 km to Kasol road, Drive 35 km to Kasol, Drive onward
itinerary.desc: Short morning descent from Malana to the motor road at Malana Dam. Drive through the Parvati Valley to Kasol — the backpacker village on the Parvati River. Lunch at Kasol. Optional hot spring visit at Manikaran (4 km further). Drive back to Bhuntar and onward to Manali or Chandigarh.
highlights: Parvati Valley drive — one of India's most scenic mountain valleys
Kasol on the Parvati River
Optional Manikaran hot springs and Sikh Gurdwara

inclusions: Private transport: Manali ↔ Naggar and Malana → Kasol → Manali

faqs.q: What is special about Malana village?
faqs.a: Malana is one of India's most fascinating villages — an isolated community in its own valley that speaks a unique language called Kanashi (unrelated to any neighbouring language) and maintains a centuries-old democratic government system called the Jamdagni Rishi Council. Malana people consider themselves descendants of Alexander the Great's army (a theory debated by historians but believed locally). Visitors are welcome but must follow strict rules: do not touch villagers, animals, or property. The village is divided into upper and lower sections. It is a genuinely extraordinary place unlike any other in India.

faqs.q: How difficult is the Chandrakhani Pass Trek?
faqs.a: Chandrakhani Pass is graded Moderate — accessible to trekkers with basic fitness who can walk 6–8 km per day on mountain terrain. The steepest section is the climb to the pass on Day 2. No prior high-altitude experience is required. The maximum altitude (3,660m) is well below the altitude where AMS becomes a serious concern for most people. This is an excellent first 'proper' Himalayan pass trek.

faqs.q: Can I combine Chandrakhani Pass with a visit to Kasol and Kheerganga?
faqs.a: Yes — the trek naturally ends near Kasol in the Parvati Valley. Many trekkers extend by 1–2 days to visit Kheerganga (hot springs at 2,950m, 12 km trek from Barshaini). Junegiri Yatra can arrange combined Chandrakhani + Kheerganga packages. WhatsApp us for combined itinerary pricing.

testimonials.quote: Chandrakhani Pass was the perfect combination of beautiful forest trekking and a genuine cultural experience at Malana. The apple orchards at the start are unlike anything on any other trek I've done. Relaxed pace, excellent guide, delicious food on trail.
testimonials.author: Neha Joshi

testimonials.quote: We were not expecting Malana to be so fascinating. The guide explained the customs and history incredibly well. The Chandrakhani pass view — Deo Tibba absolutely dominant against the blue sky — is a photograph I'll have printed forever.
testimonials.author: Arjun Singh

name: Rupin Pass Trek 8N/9D
title: Rupin Pass Trek 2026 — 8N/9D Dhaula-Sangla
meta_description: Book Rupin Pass Trek 2026 with Junegiri Yatra. 8N/9D from Dhaula (Uttarakhand) to Sangla (Himachal). Massive snow bridges in May–Jun, 4,650m pass. From ₹16,500.
keywords: Rupin Pass trek, Rupin Pass trek 2026, snow bridges trek India, Dhaula to Sangla trek, Uttarakhand Himachal crossover trek, Rupin Pass altitude, best moderate difficult treks India
hero_tagline: India's most dramatic snow bridge trek — from Uttarakhand's gorges to Himachal's Sangla Valley
destinations_short: Dhaula · Sewa · Jhaka · Dhanderas Thatch · Rupin Pass · Sangla
wa_text: Rupin Pass Trek enquiry chahiye
overview:
The Rupin Pass Trek is considered by many Himalayan trekking experts to be one of the most dramatic and visually stunning treks in India. This 9-day crossing starts in the gorges of Uttarakhand (Dhaula, in Uttarkashi district) and ends in the high-altitude apple orchards of Sangla Valley, Himachal Pradesh — a genuine multi-state crossing through utterly different landscapes.

What sets Rupin apart is its signature feature: the massive snow bridges in May and June. As the Rupin gorge narrows above the Upper Waterfall Camp, centuries of accumulated snow form enormous natural bridges spanning the river — some 20–30 metres high — that trekkers walk beneath and over. The scale is extraordinary. Later in the season (September–October), the snow bridges have melted, but the trail is drier and the landscape equally spectacular.

The route passes through remote Himalayan villages — Sewa, Jhaka, Dhanderas Thatch — that see few outsiders. The locals are predominantly from the Kinnauri and Garhwali communities, and the hospitality is genuine and warm. The final descent into Sangla Valley — famous for its apple orchards, ancient Kamru Fort, and the turquoise Baspa River — is a spectacular reward after 8 days on trail.

Junegiri Yatra manages the full logistics including transport from Haridwar to Dhaula, all camping, certified guides with Rupin Pass experience, and the return from Sangla to Chandigarh or Haridwar. This trek requires a reasonable level of fitness and prior multi-day trekking experience. The snow bridge section between Dhanderas Thatch and the Upper Waterfall Camp requires microspikes and trekking poles and is guided carefully by our experienced team.

itinerary.title: Day 1 — Haridwar to Dhaula
itinerary.meta: Drive ~280 km / 10–11 hours via Uttarkashi
itinerary.desc: Depart Haridwar in the early morning. Drive via Rishikesh, Tehri, and Uttarkashi to Dhaula village (1,980m) in the Tons Valley. Long drive through the Garhwal Himalaya foothills and mountains. Check into guesthouse, acclimatise, briefing for the trek.
highlights: Uttarkashi town — gateway to Gangotri and Yamunotri
Tons Valley river gorge scenery
Dhaula village — trek starting point at 1,980 m

itinerary.title: Day 2 — Dhaula to Sewa
itinerary.meta: Trek 11 km / Altitude gain to 2,650 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: The trail begins along the Rupin River through dense forest and river crossings. The Rupin gorge progressively narrows as you trek upstream. Sewa village (2,650m) is a small Garhwali village with traditional stone houses. Camp near the village.
highlights: Rupin River gorge — increasingly narrow and dramatic
Dense mixed forest with birch and pine
Sewa village stone houses and traditional hospitality

itinerary.title: Day 3 — Sewa to Jhaka
itinerary.meta: Trek 8 km / Altitude gain to 2,900 m / 4–5 hrs
itinerary.desc: Steady climb through forest and meadow to Jhaka village (2,900m). The village is perched on a ridge with sweeping views of the Rupin Valley below. Traditional Kinnauri-Garhwali architecture. Short day to allow acclimatisation and rest before the more demanding days ahead.
highlights: Jhaka village — ridge-top settlement with valley views
Progressive Rupin gorge views from above
Acclimatisation day at 2,900 m

itinerary.title: Day 4 — Jhaka to Dhanderas Thatch
itinerary.meta: Trek 7 km / Altitude gain to 3,500 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: The trail leaves the villages behind and enters open alpine terrain. Dhanderas Thatch is a high meadow used by Gaddi shepherds in summer. The views of the upper Rupin Valley — the peaks at the head of the valley closing in — are extraordinary. The snow bridges become visible on the far walls of the gorge for the first time. Camp at Dhanderas Thatch.
highlights: Above-treeline alpine meadow at Dhanderas Thatch (3,500 m)
First views of the upper Rupin gorge and snow bridges
Peak views closing in from all sides

itinerary.title: Day 5 — Dhanderas Thatch to Upper Waterfall Camp
itinerary.meta: Trek 6 km / Altitude gain to 4,000 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: The most visually spectacular day of the trek. The trail enters the famous snow bridge section — massive snow arches spanning the Rupin gorge, formed by avalanche snow compressed over winters. Walking beneath and over the snow bridges with the waterfall thundering through is a jaw-dropping experience. Camp at the Upper Waterfall Camp below the main Rupin waterfall.
highlights: Rupin snow bridges — the signature feature of this trek (May–June only)
Massive waterfall through the snow gorge
High camp at 4,000 m below the pass approach

itinerary.title: Day 6 — Upper Waterfall Camp over Rupin Pass to Ronti Gad
itinerary.meta: Trek 6 km / Max altitude 4,650 m / 7–8 hrs
itinerary.desc: The crux day. An early start before dawn, climbing steeply on snow and rock to the Rupin Pass at 4,650 metres. The pass is a broad saddle with views into both Uttarakhand and Himachal Pradesh. Cross into Himachal and descend carefully on the snow slope to the Ronti Gad stream. The descent is steep but the scenery changes dramatically — the barren high-altitude terrain giving way to the distant hint of the Sangla Valley ahead.
highlights: Rupin Pass summit at 4,650 m — Uttarakhand–Himachal crossover
360° high-altitude panorama from the pass
Descent into Himachal Pradesh — new terrain begins

itinerary.title: Day 7 — Ronti Gad to Sangla Kanda
itinerary.meta: Trek 8 km / Descent to 3,900 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: Descend along the Ronti Gad stream through changing landscape — high boulder fields giving way to scrub juniper and the first hints of the Kinnauri valley landscape. Sangla Kanda is a high meadow campsite in Himachal with views toward the Sangla Valley and the distant Kinner Kailash range.
highlights: Kinnauri landscape — transition to Himachal terrain
First views of Kinner Kailash range
Sangla Kanda high meadow camp

itinerary.title: Day 8 — Sangla Kanda to Sangla Village
itinerary.meta: Trek 10 km / Descent to 2,700 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: Long descent through forests and orchards to the floor of the Sangla Valley. The apple orchards of Sangla — some of India's finest — begin at around 2,800m. Sangla village itself is a beautiful Kinnauri settlement with traditional wooden architecture, Kamru Fort above, and the turquoise Baspa River running through the valley. Well-deserved rest and celebration dinner.
highlights: Sangla apple orchards — descent through a sea of fruit trees
Sangla village traditional Kinnauri wooden architecture
Kamru Fort viewpoint and Baspa River

itinerary.title: Day 9 — Sangla to Chandigarh / Haridwar
itinerary.meta: Drive ~350 km / 10–11 hours
itinerary.desc: Drive out of Sangla Valley through Reckong Peo and Rampur to the NH5 highway, then onward to Chandigarh or Haridwar depending on your onward travel. Long drive but the Kinnauri and Sutlej Valley scenery is spectacular. Trek officially complete.
highlights: Kinnauri valley and Sutlej River gorge drive
Reckong Peo — Kinnaur district headquarters
Drop at Chandigarh or Haridwar

faqs.q: When are the snow bridges visible on the Rupin Pass Trek?
faqs.a: The famous Rupin snow bridges are at their maximum size in late May and early June. By July they have typically partially collapsed, and by September they are gone. The snow bridge season (May–June) offers the most dramatic visuals but requires microspikes and extra caution. The September–October window has no snow bridges but offers dry trails, clear skies, and the Sangla apple harvest — a different but equally beautiful experience.

faqs.q: How difficult is the Rupin Pass Trek?
faqs.a: Rupin Pass is graded Moderate–Difficult. The total distance is 60+ km over 7 trekking days, reaching a maximum altitude of 4,650m. The pass crossing day (Day 6) is demanding — steep snow slope ascent starting before dawn. Prior experience of at least 2–3 multi-day treks is recommended. Good fitness, acclimatisation awareness, and the right gear (microspikes, trekking poles, layered warm clothing) are essential.

faqs.q: Is the Rupin Pass Trek in Uttarakhand or Himachal Pradesh?
faqs.a: Both — this is what makes it special. The trek starts in Uttarakhand (Dhaula, Uttarkashi district) and ends in Himachal Pradesh (Sangla Valley, Kinnaur district). The pass itself straddles the state boundary. Inner line permits for the Kinnaur district section are required and are arranged by Junegiri Yatra as part of the package.

faqs.q: What are the alternative seasons for Rupin Pass Trek?
faqs.a: Rupin Pass has two reliable windows: late May to mid-June (snow bridges, some post-monsoon residual snow, cold nights) and September to mid-October (no snow on trail, clearer skies, drier underfoot, Sangla apple harvest). The trek is closed July–August (heavy monsoon landslides on Uttarkashi roads) and November to April (pass buried under snow).

testimonials.quote: The Rupin snow bridges stopped me in my tracks. I genuinely could not believe what I was seeing — 30-metre snow arches with the river thundering underneath. Junegiri Yatra had microspikes ready, the guide knew every step, and we crossed safely. Nothing prepares you for how beautiful it is.
testimonials.author: Dhruv Sharma

testimonials.quote: Did Rupin in October — no snow bridges, but the trek was outstanding. The Sangla Valley descent through apple orchards was magical, and the Kinnauri villages are like stepping back in time. Our guide Govind was extraordinary — knowledgeable about local culture, birds, plants, everything.
testimonials.author: Ritu Agarwal

name: Kanamo Peak Trek 5N/6D
title: Kanamo Peak Trek 2026 — 5N/6D from Spiti
meta_description: Book Kanamo Peak Trek 2026. 5N/6D from Kaza, Spiti Valley. Summit India's highest non-technical peaks at 5,964m. 360° views of Spiti, Kinner Kailash, Tibet.…
keywords: Kanamo Peak trek, Kanamo Peak Spiti, Spiti Valley trek, high altitude peak India, non-technical peak India 6000m, Kaza trek, Kibber trek, Spiti trekking 2026
hero_tagline: One of India's highest non-technical peaks — 360° views of the Spiti cold desert, Kinner Kailash, and Tibet
destinations_short: Kaza · Kibber · Base Camp · Kanamo Summit
wa_text: Kanamo Peak Trek enquiry chahiye
overview:
Kanamo Peak at 5,964 metres (19,567 ft) stands as one of India's highest non-technical summit challenges — achievable without mountaineering equipment like fixed ropes or crampons, yet demanding enough to be a serious achievement for any trekker. Set in the spectacular cold desert landscape of Spiti Valley in Himachal Pradesh, Kanamo offers views that extend from the Spiti range to Kinner Kailash and clear to the high peaks of Tibet on a clear day.

The journey begins with the famous drive from Manali to Kaza via the Rohtang Pass and Kunzum La — itself one of the great high-altitude road experiences in India. Kaza (3,800m) is the administrative capital of Spiti, a small town of whitewashed buildings at the confluence of the Spiti and Pin rivers, surrounded by barren ochre mountains. Two days are spent here and at the nearby village of Kibber for acclimatisation — essential before attempting a summit above 5,900m.

The route to Kanamo base camp (4,300m) passes through the upper reaches of Kibber village — one of the world's highest inhabited villages — and into raw, boulder-strewn terrain typical of Spiti. The base camp offers stark, immensely beautiful views of the Spiti Valley far below. Summit day is long and physically demanding, gaining nearly 1,700m of altitude from base camp, but the summit views are among the most extraordinary in the Indian Himalaya — a complete 360-degree panorama of a landscape that looks more like the moon than India.

Junegiri Yatra provides experienced high-altitude guides with Spiti experience, full camping equipment, all meals, oxygen and emergency supplies, and inner line permits for the Spiti restricted area. Prior experience of trekking above 4,000m is mandatory for this trek. AMS protocol is strictly followed.

itinerary.title: Day 1 — Manali to Kaza
itinerary.meta: Drive 205 km / 8–9 hours via Rohtang and Kunzum La
itinerary.desc: Depart Manali early morning. Drive via the Rohtang Pass (3,978m) or Atal Tunnel to Gramphoo, then through the Lahaul Valley and over the Kunzum Pass (4,551m) into Spiti. Arrive in Kaza (3,800m) by late afternoon. Check into guesthouse. Light dinner, early rest for altitude adjustment. No trekking on Day 1.
highlights: Kunzum La (4,551 m) — gateway to Spiti Valley
Dramatic landscape transition: lush Kullu to barren Spiti
Kaza town at 3,800 m — first night of altitude acclimatisation

itinerary.title: Day 2 — Kaza Acclimatisation Day
itinerary.meta: Kaza town and surroundings / Altitude 3,800 m
itinerary.desc: Full acclimatisation day in Kaza. Morning walk to Key Monastery — the iconic 1,000-year-old hilltop gompa visible from across the Spiti Valley. Afternoon visit to Kibber village (4,205m) for altitude exposure. Evening briefing on AMS symptoms, summit day plan, and gear check. Light meals, good hydration, early sleep.
highlights: Key Monastery — 1,000-year-old Spiti hilltop gompa
Kibber village at 4,205 m for acclimatisation exposure
AMS briefing and gear check

itinerary.title: Day 3 — Kaza to Kibber, Trek to Base Camp
itinerary.meta: Drive to Kibber, Trek 6 km / Altitude gain to 4,300 m / 3–4 hrs
itinerary.desc: Drive from Kaza to Kibber village (4,205m). Begin trek from the upper edge of Kibber through boulder fields and high-altitude scrub desert to the Kanamo base camp at 4,300m. The landscape is elemental — nothing but rock, sky, and the distant peaks. Set up camp. Rest afternoon. Altitude acclimatisation hike of 200m above camp.
highlights: Kibber village — one of the world's highest inhabited villages
First look at Kanamo Peak from approach trail
Base camp at 4,300 m with full Spiti Valley panorama

itinerary.title: Day 4 — Base Camp Acclimatisation Hike
itinerary.meta: High camp acclimatisation / Altitude 4,300–4,600 m
itinerary.desc: Full day for acclimatisation at and above base camp. Morning hike to 4,600m, then return to base camp to sleep low. This 'climb high, sleep low' pattern is essential for preparing the body for summit day. Afternoon rest, good nutrition, and hydration. Guide reviews summit day route and weather forecast. Early bed — 3am wake-up tomorrow.
highlights: Climb high, sleep low acclimatisation protocol
Scouting the lower summit route
Weather window assessment for summit day

itinerary.title: Day 5 — Summit Push to Kanamo Peak (5,964m) and Return
itinerary.meta: Trek to summit and return / Max altitude 5,964 m / 10–12 hrs
itinerary.desc: 3am wake-up. Depart camp in the dark with head torches. The trail climbs steep scree, boulder fields, and in early season some snow slopes. The final ridge walk to the summit requires care but no technical equipment. At the summit (5,964m), on a clear day the view extends to Kinner Kailash, the Bara Shigri glacier, and peaks across the Tibetan border. Return carefully to base camp, then drive back to Kaza for the night.
highlights: Kanamo summit at 5,964 m — highest point of the trek
360° views of Spiti, Kinner Kailash, and Tibet
Return to Kaza for celebratory dinner

itinerary.title: Day 6 — Kaza to Manali / Chandigarh
itinerary.meta: Drive 205–350 km / 9–12 hours
itinerary.desc: After a well-deserved breakfast in Kaza, drive back to Manali or Chandigarh depending on your onward travel. Retrace the Kunzum La and Rohtang route. Trek and peak summit complete. Transfer to hotel or bus stand in Manali.
highlights: Return via Kunzum La and Rohtang Pass
Final views of the Spiti Valley
Trek concludes on arrival in Manali

faqs.q: Is Kanamo Peak suitable for first-time trekkers?
faqs.a: No — Kanamo Peak is graded Difficult and requires prior high-altitude trekking experience. You should have completed at least two multi-day treks above 4,000m before attempting Kanamo. The summit at 5,964m exposes you to serious altitude risks including HAPE and HACE. The trek involves steep scree and boulder ascent in pre-dawn darkness. Trekkers who are physically fit but have no high-altitude experience should first complete Hamta Pass (4,270m) or Bhrigu Lake (4,300m) before considering Kanamo.

faqs.q: What are the AMS risks on Kanamo Peak and how does Junegiri handle them?
faqs.a: At 5,964m, AMS (Acute Mountain Sickness) is a real risk, and HAPE (High Altitude Pulmonary Edema) becomes possible. Junegiri Yatra's protocol includes: mandatory 2-day acclimatisation in Kaza, 'climb high, sleep low' protocol on Day 4, pulse oximeter monitoring at every camp, oxygen cylinder available at base camp, and guides trained in high-altitude first aid and emergency descent procedures. Any guest with SpO2 below 80% or severe symptoms will not be permitted to continue to the summit.

faqs.q: When is the best time to trek Kanamo Peak?
faqs.a: The best season for Kanamo Peak is June to September. June has the clearest skies and some residual snow. July–August is fully open with stable weather. September has crisp visibility and the Spiti landscape turns golden. The Manali–Kaza highway opens in late May (after Rohtang Pass clears) and closes in November. October can still be attempted but nights become very cold and the highway closure risk increases.

faqs.q: What makes Spiti Valley unique as a trekking destination?
faqs.a: Spiti is one of the most extraordinary landscapes in Asia — a high-altitude cold desert at 3,800–4,500m that receives less than 200mm of rain per year. The scenery looks like a Tibetan plateau: ochre and rust-coloured mountains, turquoise rivers, whitewashed monasteries clinging to cliffsides, and sky so blue it seems artificially saturated. The culture is Tibetan Buddhist — ancient gompas (Key, Tabo, Dhankar), prayer flags, and mani walls everywhere. Wildlife includes snow leopard, Himalayan ibex, and bar-headed geese. It is unlike any other trekking region in India.

testimonials.quote: Standing on Kanamo summit at 5,964m with Tibet visible on the horizon — I cannot describe what that feels like. Two years of trekking led to this moment. Junegiri Yatra's guide, Tenzin, was outstanding. The AMS protocol was serious and thorough — I felt safe the entire time.
testimonials.author: Vikash Pandey

testimonials.quote: The Spiti Valley drive alone is worth it — but standing on top of Kanamo with the entire cold desert spread below us was another level entirely. The acclimatisation plan was well-designed and I had zero AMS. This is the best trekking experience of my life.
testimonials.author: Shruti Malhotra

name: Pin Parvati Pass Trek 10N/11D
title: Pin Parvati Pass Trek 2026 — 10N/11D
meta_description: Book Pin Parvati Pass Trek 2026. 10N/11D, cross from lush Parvati Valley to barren Spiti at 5,319m. Remote, glacial, challenging. India's best multi-day…
keywords: Pin Parvati Pass trek, Pin Parvati Pass 2026, Kullu to Spiti trek, Kheerganga to Kaza trek, hardest treks India, remote treks Himachal Pradesh, Pin Parvati Pass altitude, glacier trek India
hero_tagline: India's most remote crossover — from the lush Parvati Valley to the barren cold desert of Spiti across a 5,319m glacial pass
destinations_short: Kheerganga · Tunda Bhuj · Mantalai Lake · Pin Parvati Pass · Pin Valley · Mudh · Kaza
wa_text: Pin Parvati Pass Trek enquiry chahiye
overview:
The Pin Parvati Pass Trek is one of the most remote, challenging, and breathtaking treks in the entire Indian Himalaya. This 11-day crossing begins in the lush, forested Parvati Valley of Kullu district and ends in the stark, lunar landscape of Spiti Valley's Pin Valley National Park — a complete transformation of terrain, culture, and climate over 90+ kilometres of trail.

The Pin Parvati Pass stands at 5,319 metres (17,457 ft) — higher than most European peaks — and is only reliably accessible for 10 weeks per year (July to September) when the glacier approach is stable. The pass sits on the boundary of the Kullu and Lahaul-Spiti districts and requires careful navigation, glacier crossing experience, and absolute fitness. This is not a trek for beginners.

The approach from the Parvati Valley side is one of the most beautiful in Himachal: starting at the famous hot springs and meadow of Kheerganga, the trail climbs through increasingly remote terrain past Tunda Bhuj, Thakur Kuan, and the remote Hindu pilgrimage site of Mantalai Lake (4,116m) — believed to be a sacred lake of Lord Shiva. Above Mantalai, the trail enters permanent glacier and snowfield territory, demanding technical awareness but no roped climbing.

The descent into Pin Valley is dramatic — a stark, nearly vertical landscape of grey scree and silver glacial streams. The Pin Valley National Park is one of the last habitats of the snow leopard in India, and sightings, while rare, are possible. Villages like Mudh in Pin Valley are among the most isolated in Himachal Pradesh, accessible only via this trek or a long dirt road from Kaza.

Junegiri Yatra provides the full logistics team: experienced guide with Pin Parvati experience, cook, porter (for shared equipment), camping gear, and all meals. The trek requires prior experience of at least two 5-day high-altitude treks above 4,000m. Weather windows are critical and we monitor forecasts daily. This trek is only offered July through September.

itinerary.title: Day 1 — Manali/Bhuntar to Barshaini, Trek to Kheerganga
itinerary.meta: Drive to Barshaini, Trek 12 km / Altitude gain to 2,950 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: Drive from Bhuntar (30 km from Manali) up the Parvati Valley to Barshaini. Begin trek along the Parvati River through Nakthan and Kalga villages, climbing through pine forest to the famous Kheerganga meadow. Hot springs, camping meadow, and the first distant view of the high peaks ahead. Camp at Kheerganga.
highlights: Parvati Valley trek start — forest and river trail
Kheerganga hot springs and meadow at 2,950 m
First glimpse of the high Parvati Valley peaks

itinerary.title: Day 2 — Kheerganga to Tunda Bhuj
itinerary.meta: Trek 7 km / Altitude gain to 3,400 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: The valley narrows significantly above Kheerganga. Trail through birch forest and boulder fields. The Parvati River becomes smaller and more powerful as you approach its headwaters. Tunda Bhuj is a rough camping spot with the valley walls closing in dramatically above.
highlights: Narrow upper Parvati Valley — dramatic walls on both sides
Tunda Bhuj camping spot at 3,400 m
Last major forest section of the trek

itinerary.title: Day 3 — Tunda Bhuj to Thakur Kuan
itinerary.meta: Trek 8 km / Altitude gain to 3,750 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: Continued ascent through increasingly open terrain above the treeline. The trail crosses several mountain streams. Thakur Kuan is a cairn-marked campsite at 3,750m with the high peaks of the Parvati glacier visible ahead. Views back down the Parvati Valley are sweeping and dramatic.
highlights: Above treeline — open boulder and meadow terrain
First views of the Parvati glacier ahead
Thakur Kuan campsite at 3,750 m

itinerary.title: Day 4 — Thakur Kuan to Mantalai Lake
itinerary.meta: Trek 6 km / Altitude gain to 4,116 m / 4–5 hrs
itinerary.desc: Approach to the sacred Mantalai Lake (4,116m) — a high-altitude glacial lake revered as the source of the Parvati River and a holy site of Lord Shiva. The lake is stark and beautiful, surrounded by rockfall and glacier debris. Prayers and a moment of quiet at this remote pilgrimage site. Camp beside the lake.
highlights: Mantalai Lake at 4,116 m — sacred source of Parvati River
Remote Shiva pilgrimage site
Glacial lake surrounded by peaks

itinerary.title: Day 5 — Mantalai Lake Rest and Acclimatisation
itinerary.meta: Mantalai Lake / Altitude 4,116 m
itinerary.desc: Full rest and acclimatisation day at Mantalai Lake. Morning acclimatisation hike to 4,400m above the lake, then return to sleep at 4,116m. Pulse oximeter checks. Guide briefing on glacier crossing protocol and route above the lake. Weather assessment. Early sleep — critical before the next demanding days.
highlights: Acclimatisation hike above Mantalai Lake
Glacier route briefing and gear check
Explore the sacred lake shore

itinerary.title: Day 6 — Mantalai Lake to Approach Camp
itinerary.meta: Trek 5 km on glacier approaches / Altitude gain to 4,700 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: Enter permanent glacier territory above Mantalai Lake. The trail crosses moraine fields and the lower Parvati glacier. Navigation requires an experienced guide familiar with seasonal route changes. Camp at the high approach camp (4,700m) below the final pass wall — the coldest night of the trek. Temperatures can drop to -15°C.
highlights: Entry into permanent glacier terrain
High approach camp at 4,700 m
Pin Parvati Pass visible directly above the camp

itinerary.title: Day 7 — Approach Camp over Pin Parvati Pass to Descent Camp
itinerary.meta: Trek 6 km / Max altitude 5,319 m / 9–10 hrs
itinerary.desc: The most demanding day. 3am start in the cold and dark. Climb the glacier and final snow slope to the Pin Parvati Pass at 5,319 metres. The pass is narrow with vertical drops on both sides — careful footing essential. From the top, Pin Valley opens below in stark contrast: grey and bone-dry where the Parvati side was green. Careful descent on the Spiti side, steep and loose, to the first descent camp.
highlights: Pin Parvati Pass summit at 5,319 m — Kullu–Spiti boundary
Glacier and snow ascent in pre-dawn conditions
Dramatic landscape transformation at the pass

itinerary.title: Day 8 — Descent into Pin Valley
itinerary.meta: Trek 8 km / Descent to 3,900 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: Continued descent into Pin Valley through dramatic grey scree and boulder landscape. The Pin River appears as a silver thread far below. Enter Pin Valley National Park — prime snow leopard habitat. The silence and barrenness of this valley is striking after the lush Parvati side. Camp on the valley floor.
highlights: Pin Valley National Park — snow leopard habitat
Dramatic descent through grey scree and moraine
First Spiti-side camp on the valley floor

itinerary.title: Day 9 — Pin Valley to Mudh Village
itinerary.meta: Trek 10 km / Descent to 3,650 m / 5–6 hrs
itinerary.desc: Trek down the Pin Valley to Mudh — one of Himachal's most remote inhabited villages, accessible only by this trek or a rough dirt road from Kaza that is often washed out. Traditional Kinnauri/Spitian architecture, prayer flags, and warm hospitality. This is the last trekking day. Rest and celebration at Mudh.
highlights: Mudh village — one of India's most isolated settlements
Traditional Spitian architecture and culture
Last day of trekking on the trail

itinerary.title: Day 10 — Mudh to Kaza
itinerary.meta: Drive ~55 km / 2–3 hours on mountain road
itinerary.desc: Drive from Mudh out of Pin Valley to the main Spiti Valley road and into Kaza. The dirt road from Mudh is rough and scenic — crossing the Pin River multiple times. Arrive Kaza for the night. Well-deserved rest, hot meal, and shower.
highlights: Pin Valley drive — rough mountain road to Kaza
First night in a proper bed since Day 1
Kaza celebration dinner

itinerary.meta: Drive 205–350 km / 9–12 hours
itinerary.desc: Final drive back to Manali via Kunzum La and Rohtang Pass, or to Chandigarh via Reckong Peo and Rampur. Trek officially complete — one of the most challenging and rewarding multi-day crossovers in the Indian Himalaya. Transfer to hotel or transport hub.
highlights: Final Spiti Valley views on the drive out
Return via Kunzum La or Kinnaur route
Trek complete on arrival in Manali or Chandigarh

faqs.q: How remote and difficult is the Pin Parvati Pass Trek?
faqs.a: Pin Parvati is one of India's most demanding treks. It is remote (no mobile signal, no villages for 5+ days in the middle section), long (10 days of trekking), and high (5,319m pass with glacier approach). The middle section between Mantalai Lake and Mudh village has no escape route and no possibility of helicopter evacuation in bad weather. This is a serious undertaking — not appropriate for casual trekkers. Prior completion of at least two 5-day treks above 4,000m is required.

faqs.q: When is the Pin Parvati Pass open?
faqs.a: The pass is typically accessible from mid-July to mid-September only — a window of approximately 9–10 weeks per year. Before July, the glacier above Mantalai Lake is too steep and the snow conditions unstable. After mid-September, early winter snowfall makes the pass dangerous. Junegiri Yatra only operates this trek during confirmed open windows after guide-team reconnaissance. We do not operate in June or October regardless of conditions.

faqs.q: What wildlife can I see on the Pin Parvati Trek?
faqs.a: The Pin Valley section passes through Pin Valley National Park, one of India's best snow leopard habitats. Snow leopard sightings are rare but not impossible — we have had guest sightings in September. More commonly seen: Himalayan ibex (in large herds on the Pin Valley cliffs), bar-headed geese (at Mantalai Lake), red fox, Himalayan griffon vulture, and in the Parvati Valley, langur monkeys and monal pheasant. The biodiversity jump between the two valleys is remarkable.

faqs.q: Is there any technical mountaineering required on Pin Parvati Pass?
faqs.a: No fixed ropes or climbing equipment are required, but the pass approach involves glacier and snow slope travel that requires microspikes and trekking poles (both provided by Junegiri Yatra). The glacier section requires your guide to lead and set the route — this is why Pin Parvati is only attempted with an experienced, route-familiar guide. The summit day is physically and mentally demanding but not technically dangerous with the right team and conditions.

testimonials.quote: Ten days to cross from one world to another — I've never experienced anything like Pin Parvati. Our guide had crossed Pin Parvati four times and knew every metre of the route.

wa_text: Hi Junegiri Yatra! I'm interested in the Everest Base Camp Trek 13N/14D. Please share availability, fixed departures and pricing.
overview:
The Everest Base Camp trek is the most iconic high-altitude walk on Earth — a two-week journey through the Khumbu region of Nepal that carries you from the airstrip at Lukla all the way to the foot of Sagarmatha (Mount Everest, 8,849m). Reaching the base camp at 5,364m and the viewpoint of Kala Patthar at 5,545m, this is a trek measured not in technical climbing but in patience, acclimatization and quiet endurance.

The trail threads through the homeland of the Sherpa people, passing prayer-wheel-lined villages, swaying suspension bridges strung with prayer flags, and centuries-old monasteries. The bustling mountain town of Namche Bazaar serves as the cultural and trading hub of the region, while the monastery at Tengboche offers one of the most photographed mountain backdrops in the world, framed by Ama Dablam.

Our 14-day itinerary is built around safe, gradual ascent. Two dedicated acclimatization days — at Namche Bazaar and Dingboche — give your body time to adjust to thinning air, dramatically reducing the risk of altitude sickness and giving you the best possible chance of standing on Kala Patthar at dawn with Everest glowing gold above you.

Every Junegiri Yatra Everest departure is supported by experienced local Sherpa guides, comfortable teahouse lodging, full board on the trail, and round-trip flights between Kathmandu and Lukla. We handle the permits, the logistics and the emergency protocols — you focus on the walk of a lifetime.

faq.q: How fit do I need to be for Everest Base Camp?
faq.a: You should be able to walk 5–7 hours a day on consecutive days carrying a light daypack. No technical climbing is involved, but the altitude makes everything harder. We recommend 6–8 weeks of cardio and hill training before departure. Regular trekkers of average-to-good fitness complete EBC successfully every season.

faq.q: Is altitude sickness a risk on this trek?
faq.a: Altitude is the single biggest challenge on EBC. Our 14-day itinerary includes two full acclimatization days and a deliberately gradual ascent profile to minimize the risk. Your guide carries an oximeter and monitors the group daily. Hydration, slow pacing and informing your guide of any symptoms early are the keys to a safe summit.

faq.q: What about the Lukla flight?
faq.a: Round-trip flights between Kathmandu and Lukla are included. Lukla is a mountain airstrip and flights are weather-dependent — occasional delays are normal. We build buffer into our planning and recommend keeping a spare day in Kathmandu before your international flight home.

faq.q: When is the best time to trek to Everest Base Camp?
faq.a: The two prime windows are spring (March to early June) and autumn (late September to November). Autumn offers the clearest skies and stable weather; spring brings warmer days and blooming rhododendrons. Winter is very cold but quiet, and the monsoon (July–August) is best avoided.

faq.q: What accommodation can I expect?
faq.a: You'll stay in teahouses (mountain lodges) run by local families, on a twin-sharing basis. Rooms are simple and clean with shared dining halls heated by a central stove. In Kathmandu you stay in a comfortable 3-star hotel for the first and last nights.

faq.q: Do you run fixed departures or private treks?
faq.a: We offer both. Join one of our scheduled group departures, or book a fully private trek on your own dates with family or friends. WhatsApp us with your preferred window and group size for a custom quote.

travel_guide:
The Complete Guide to the Everest Base Camp Trek

The Everest Base Camp trek sits at the top of almost every serious trekker's list, and for good reason. Over roughly 130 kilometres of walking across two weeks, it delivers an unmatched combination of mountain scenery, Sherpa culture and personal achievement — all without requiring ropes, crampons or technical climbing skill.

Why the 14-day itinerary matters

Shorter EBC itineraries exist, but they cut acclimatization days to save time — and that is exactly where altitude sickness creeps in. Our schedule includes rest-and-climb days at both Namche Bazaar (3,440m) and Dingboche (4,410m), following the golden rule of climb high, sleep low. This gives your body the time it needs and significantly raises your odds of standing on Kala Patthar at sunrise.

The highlights you'll remember

Beyond the base camp itself, the trek's defining moments include the first distant view of Everest above Namche, the monastery and Ama Dablam backdrop at Tengboche, the moving climbers' memorials above Thukla, and the dawn panorama from Kala Patthar (5,545m) — the highest and finest viewpoint of the journey.

Getting there from India

Most Indian trekkers fly into Kathmandu, where Indian nationals do not require a visa. From Kathmandu, the included mountain flight to Lukla places you at the trailhead. We coordinate the permits, flights and logistics so you can travel from anywhere in India with a single point of contact in Haridwar.

Booking with Junegiri Yatra

Every departure includes experienced Sherpa guides, porter support, full board on the trail and 24/7 operational backup. WhatsApp our team for fixed-departure dates, private-group pricing and a personalized packing list.

name: Sar Pass Trek 4N/5D
title: Sar Pass Trek 5D — Kasol, Parvati Valley
